input_row.title: Ensure that 'Auditing' is set to 'On'
input_row.rationale: The Azure platform allows a SQL server to be created as a service. Enabling auditing at the server level ensures that all existing and newly created databases on the SQL server instance are audited. Auditing policy applied on the SQL database does not override auditing policy and settings applied on the particular SQL server where the database is hosted. Auditing tracks database events and writes them to an audit log in the Azure storage account. It also helps to maintain regulatory compliance, understand database activity, and gain insight into discrepancies and anomalies that could indicate business concerns or suspected security violations.
input_row.audit: From Azure Portal 1. Go to SQL servers 2. For each server instance 3. Click on Auditing 4. Ensure that Enable Azure SQL Auditing is set to On From PowerShell Get the list of all SQL Servers Get-AzSqlServer For each Server Get-AzSqlServerAudit -ResourceGroupName <ResourceGroupName> -ServerName <SQLServerName> Ensure that BlobStorageTargetState, EventHubTargetState, or LogAnalyticsTargetState is set to Enabled.  From Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: a6fb4358-5bf4-4ad7-ba82-2cd2f41ce5e9 - Name: 'Auditing on SQL server should be enabled'
input_row.remediation: From Azure Portal 1. Go to SQL servers 2. Select the SQL server instance 3. Under Security, click Auditing 4. Click the toggle next to Enable Azure SQL Auditing 5. Select an Audit log destination 6. Click Save From PowerShell Get the list of all SQL Servers Get-AzSqlServer For each Server, enable auditing and set the retention for at least 90 days. Log Analytics Example Set-AzSqlServerAudit -ResourceGroupName <resource group name> -ServerName <SQL Server name> -RetentionInDays <Number of Days to retain the audit logs, should be 90days minimum> -LogAnalyticsTargetState Enabled - WorkspaceResourceId "/subscriptions/<subscription ID>/resourceGroups/insights- integration/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/<workspace name>  Event Hub Example Set-AzSqlServerAudit -ResourceGroupName "<resource group name>" -ServerName "<SQL Server name>" -EventHubTargetState Enabled -EventHubName "<Event Hub name>" -EventHubAuthorizationRuleResourceId "<Event Hub Authorization Rule Resource ID>"  Blob Storage Example Set-AzSqlServerAudit -ResourceGroupName "<resource group name>" -ServerName "<SQL Server name>" -BlobStorageTargetState Enabled -StorageAccountResourceId "/subscriptions/<subscription_ID>/resourceGroups/<Resource_Group>/providers/M icrosoft.Stora ge/storageAccounts/<Storage Account name>" Default Value: By default, Enable Azure SQL Auditing is set to Off. References: 1. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security-center/security-center-enable- auditing-on-sql-servers 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/azurerm.sql/get- azurermsqlserverauditing?view=azurermps-5.2.0 3. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/azurerm.sql/set- azurermsqlserverauditingpolicy?view=azurermps-5.2.0 4. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/sql-database/sql-database-auditing 5. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-logging-threat- detection#lt-3-enable-logging-for-security-investigation Additional Information: • A server policy applies to all existing and newly created databases on the server. • If server blob auditing is enabled, it always applies to the database. The database will be audited, regardless of the database auditing settings. Auditing type table is already deprecated leaving only type blob available. • Enabling blob auditing on the database, in addition to enabling it on the server, does not override or change any of the settings of the server blob auditing. Both audits will exist side by side. In other words, the database is audited twice in parallel; once by the server policy and once by the database policy.
input_row.reference: 1. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/security-center/security-center-enable- auditing-on-sql-servers 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/azurerm.sql/get- azurermsqlserverauditing?view=azurermps-5.2.0 3. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/azurerm.sql/set- azurermsqlserverauditingpolicy?view=azurermps-5.2.0 4. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/sql-database/sql-database-auditing 5. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-logging-threat- detection#lt-3-enable-logging-for-security-investigation Additional Information: • A server policy applies to all existing and newly created databases on the server. • If server blob auditing is enabled, it always applies to the database. The database will be audited, regardless of the database auditing settings. Auditing type table is already deprecated leaving only type blob available. • Enabling blob auditing on the database, in addition to enabling it on the server, does not override or change any of the settings of the server blob auditing. Both audits will exist side by side. In other words, the database is audited twice in parallel; once by the server policy and once by the database policy.

first_opinion.response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:** 
- The audit field provides clear instructions to check the Azure SQL Server auditing configuration using Azure PowerShell commands. 
- It mentions specific commands that a CSPM tool can utilize.
- Azure APIs/CLI exist for verifying SQL Server configurations.
- This control can be fully automated without requiring manual inspection or judgment.

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_sql_server_auditing_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
- The automated program would check the following:
  - Use the Azure PowerShell command `Get-AzSqlServer` to enumerate all SQL Servers in the Azure environment.
  - For each SQL Server, use `Get-AzSqlServerAudit -ResourceGroupName <ResourceGroupName> -ServerName <SQLServerName>` to retrieve the auditing configuration settings.
  - Validate the settings for `BlobStorageTargetState`, `EventHubTargetState`, and `LogAnalyticsTargetState` to ensure they are all set to `Enabled`.
  
- Clear Pass/Fail criteria:
  - Pass: Both `BlobStorageTargetState`, `EventHubTargetState`, and `LogAnalyticsTargetState` are set to `Enabled`.
  - Fail: If any of these settings are not enabled, the check should return a fail status.

- Example API endpoint or CLI command:
  - `Get-AzSqlServerAudit -ResourceGroupName "<YourResourceGroupName>" -ServerName "<YourSQLServerName>"` would list the audit settings for a specified Azure SQL Server, which the automated tool can parse to assess compliance.

second_opinion.response:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. Azure provides comprehensive APIs, CLI commands, and PowerShell cmdlets that can be used to automate the checking of SQL Server auditing configurations. The use of `Get-AzSqlServer` and `Get-AzSqlServerAudit` is appropriate for this purpose.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8
- The automation details are mostly correct. The use of `Get-AzSqlServerAudit` to retrieve auditing settings is technically accurate. However, the description could include additional details on how to handle different configurations, such as when auditing is set at the database level instead of the server level.
- The program name follows the convention: azure_sql_server_auditing_enabled, which is clear and descriptive.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- Consideration should be given to scenarios where auditing might be configured at the database level rather than the server level. The assessment should include checks for `Get-AzSqlDatabaseAudit` to ensure comprehensive coverage.
- The audit should also verify that auditing is not only enabled but also correctly configured to meet organizational requirements, such as specific event categories being logged.
- Include error handling and logging mechanisms in the automation script to capture any issues encountered during execution.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-aligned with Azure capabilities and security best practices, and the automation approach is sound.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Modify: Enhance the automation details to include checks for database-level auditing using `Get-AzSqlDatabaseAudit` and ensure that the auditing configuration meets specific organizational requirements. This will provide a more comprehensive audit coverage.

final_decision.response:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** Both AI opinions agree that the auditing configuration for Azure SQL Servers can be verified using Azure PowerShell commands, specifically `Get-AzSqlServer` and `Get-AzSqlServerAudit`. These commands allow for the retrieval of auditing settings, which can be automated to ensure compliance with the control requirements. Azure provides comprehensive APIs and PowerShell cmdlets that facilitate this automation, making manual inspection unnecessary.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

--- IF AUTOMATED ---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
azure_sql_server_auditing_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **Azure Resource/Service:** SQL Servers
- **API/CLI Method:** Use `Get-AzSqlServer` to list all SQL Servers and `Get-AzSqlServerAudit` to check auditing settings.
- **Properties to Check:** `BlobStorageTargetState`, `EventHubTargetState`, `LogAnalyticsTargetState`
- **Pass Criteria:** All target states (`BlobStorageTargetState`, `EventHubTargetState`, `LogAnalyticsTargetState`) are set to `Enabled`.
- **Fail Criteria:** Any of the target states are not set to `Enabled`.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** There were no disagreements between the AI opinions. Both agreed on the feasibility of automation using Azure's available tools and APIs.
